description: Two pedagogical marimo notebooks for Module 3 of Applied Soft Computing. Students interact with LLMs directly to build intuition for prompt engineering and agentic AI systems.
problem_statement: Students learning agentic coding need hands-on, interactive experiences that show how prompt structure and agent architecture affect LLM behavior. Static lecture notes do not let students experiment in real time. We need executable notebooks where students can toggle prompt components on/off, see output change live, and build progressively more complex agents.
solution: Two marimo notebooks, each executable via `uvx`, that connect to ollama (local or remote). Notebook 1 teaches prompt engineering through live component toggling and structured student tasks. Notebook 2 teaches the ReAct pattern and context engineering via a working LangChain agent over the Titanic dataset and a multi-agent literature verification demo.
user_stories: As a student, I want to run the notebook with a single `uvx` command so that I do not need to set up a Python environment manually.
As a student, I want to type a litellm model string (e.g. `ollama/llama3.2` or `openai/gpt-4o`) in a UI config panel so that I can switch providers without editing code.
As a student, I want to enter an optional API key and base URL in the config panel so that I can connect to remote or self-hosted endpoints.
As a student, I want the config panel values to propagate automatically to all cells so that I only need to configure the model once per session.
As a student, I want to toggle each prompt component (instruction, data, format, persona, context) on and off individually so that I can isolate the effect of each component.
As a student, I want to see the assembled prompt update in real time as I toggle components so that I understand exactly what is being sent to the model.
As a student, I want to send the assembled prompt and see the model response so that I can observe how each component changes the output.
As a student, I want to control the number of few-shot examples with a slider so that I can compare zero-shot, one-shot, two-shot, and five-shot behavior on the same task.
As a student, I want to shuffle the order of few-shot examples and re-run so that I observe output instability caused by example ordering.
As a student, I want to run a logic puzzle in direct-answer mode so that I get a baseline response to compare against.
As a student, I want to run the same puzzle with chain-of-thought enabled so that I can see whether intermediate reasoning steps improve or hurt the final answer.
As a student, I want to write a prompt that instructs the LLM to generate 200 numbers following N(0,1) so that I practice specification-driven prompting.
As a student, I want the notebook to extract numbers from the LLM response automatically so that I can focus on crafting the prompt rather than parsing.
As a student, I want to see a histogram of the extracted numbers overlaid with the true Gaussian PDF so that I can visually verify whether my prompt succeeded.
As a student, I want to see a KS-test p-value with a pass/fail indicator so that I have an objective measure of my prompt's effectiveness.
As a student, I want to see the full ReAct trace (Thought, Action, Observation) printed step by step so that I understand how the agent reasons before acting.
As a student, I want to ask natural language questions to the pre-built Titanic data detective agent so that I see how tools chain together across multiple observations.
As a student, I want to add a cross-tabulation tool to the Titanic agent so that I practice writing tool docstrings that guide the LLM accurately.
As a student, I want to add a filter-and-count tool to the Titanic agent so that I extend the agent's capability for conditional queries.
As a student, I want to pose a multi-hop question to the extended agent and verify its answer against a ground-truth cell so that I know whether my tool design was correct.
As a student, I want to watch a single monolithic agent attempt to verify five references at once so that I see where context confusion causes errors.
As a student, I want to watch five isolated sub-agents each verify one reference in parallel so that I see how context isolation improves accuracy.
As an instructor, I want each notebook section to be a self-contained marimo cell block with explanatory prose so that students can read and run the notebook incrementally.
As an instructor, I want each student task to have a clearly marked callout block so that students can identify where the exercise begins.
As an instructor, I want each student task to include a visible first hint so that students are not stuck without any guidance.
As an instructor, I want each student task to have a programmatic success criterion so that students receive immediate objective feedback.
As an instructor, I want the default model to work with a local ollama installation so that students without paid API keys can complete all exercises.

notebooks.title: Prompt Engineering: The Art of Talking to LLMs
notebooks.subtitle: How phrasing shapes probability — and what you can do about it
learning_goals: LLMs sample from probability distributions; phrasing shapes the distribution.
A well-structured prompt has five components: Instruction, Data, Format, Persona, and Context.
Few-shot examples activate patterns but are sensitive to example order.
Chain-of-thought reasoning forces intermediate steps and improves complex task performance.
JSON schema constraints are more reliable than asking for structured output in plain text.
Uncertainty permission reduces hallucination by allowing the model to say 'I don't know'.

runtime.notes: Use `litellm` for all LLM calls. litellm provides a unified interface across ollama, OpenAI, Anthropic, and any other provider via a single `litellm.completion()` call. The model string (e.g. `ollama/llama3.2`, `openai/gpt-4o`) determines the provider; no other configuration is required.

llm_config.description: A marimo UI panel at the top of the notebook with three fields. The model string uses litellm's provider/model format, which determines the backend without any additional routing code. The config state is a marimo reactive variable; all subsequent cells depend on it and re-run automatically when it changes.

fields.name: model
fields.label: Model (litellm format, e.g. ollama/llama3.2, openai/gpt-4o, anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022)

fields.name: api_key
fields.label: API key (leave blank for local ollama)

fields.name: api_base
fields.label: API base URL (only needed for custom endpoints)

sections.title: Why Prompts Matter: LLMs as Probability Samplers
sections.content: Short conceptual intro: LLMs predict the next token from a probability distribution. Phrasing shifts the distribution. A different word can open or close entire branches of the output space. Interactive cell: two text boxes, identical task, one poorly phrased and one well phrased. Student runs both and compares.

sections.title: The Five Building Blocks
sections.content: Five toggle switches (marimo checkboxes): Instruction, Data, Format, Persona, Context. A text preview panel shows the assembled prompt in real time. A 'Send' button submits to the model. The response renders below. Students toggle components on and off, press Send each time, and observe the response shift. Each component has a default text that populates when toggled on. The section ends with a reflection question: which component had the largest effect on the response?

sections.title: Few-Shot Learning: Examples as Activators
sections.content: Task: sentiment classification (positive/negative). A slider controls the number of examples shown (0, 1, 2, 5). A 'Shuffle order' button randomizes the order of the examples. The model's classification is shown after each run. A live table records each run's (n_examples, order_seed, prediction) so students build up a small dataset showing order sensitivity. Ends with a callout warning about using few-shot examples in production.

sections.title: Chain-of-Thought: Thinking Out Loud
sections.content: A moderately hard logic puzzle (e.g., a multi-step river crossing or a simple arithmetical word problem that requires intermediate steps). A toggle switches between direct-answer mode and chain-of-thought mode. Both modes show the full response. Students compare: does CoT give the right answer? Are the reasoning steps always faithful? Ends with a caveat callout: CoT reasoning can be plausible but unfaithful.

sections.title: Structured Output: JSON Schema Constraints
sections.content: Two modes: (1) ask for JSON in the prompt text only; (2) pass a Pydantic model as the response_format schema. Students run a data extraction task (extract name, date, and amount from a fake invoice string) in both modes and observe parse reliability. The cell validates the JSON and shows a green/red success indicator.

sections.title: Hallucination and Uncertainty Permission
sections.content: A toggle adds the phrase 'If you are not certain, say so' to the system prompt. The task asks about a fabricated academic paper title and author. Students compare the response with and without uncertainty permission and observe whether the model fabricates a plausible-sounding abstract or admits uncertainty.

sections.title: Student Task 1: Gaussian by Prompt
sections.description: Students craft a prompt that makes the LLM generate exactly 200 numbers sampled from a standard normal distribution N(0,1). The notebook provides an empty prompt editor, a 'Run' button, a parser that extracts numbers from the LLM response (strips non-numeric text), and a matplotlib histogram overlaid with the true Gaussian PDF. Success criterion: the histogram visually matches the bell curve and the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test p-value is above 0.05.
sections.hint: The LLM has no random number generator. It will approximate from its training distribution. Be extremely specific: tell it exactly how many numbers should fall in each bin (e.g., for N(0,1) with bins [-3,-2,-1,0,1,2,3], the expected counts for 200 samples are approximately [1, 5, 27, 68, 54, 27, 5, 1, ...] — adjust to your binning). The more precise your specification, the more the output looks Gaussian.
sections.extension: Try changing the target distribution to N(2, 0.5). How does your prompt need to change?

sections.title: Student Task 2: When Does Chain-of-Thought Fail?
sections.description: Students are given three puzzles of increasing difficulty (a trivial arithmetic problem, a medium logic problem, and a hard combinatorics problem). They run each with and without CoT and record in a table: (puzzle, mode, correct_answer, model_answer, is_correct). Students write a one-paragraph interpretation: for which puzzle types does CoT help most? When does it introduce errors?
sections.hint: Pay attention to whether the intermediate reasoning steps are actually used to reach the final answer, or whether the final answer appears before the reasoning is complete.

sections.title: Student Task 3: Few-Shot Order Sensitivity
sections.description: Students are given four labeled sentiment examples (two positive, two negative). They run the model 10 times with the same 4 examples but in randomized order, recording the prediction for the same test sentence each time. They compute the fraction of runs that predicted each class and compare to a baseline of zero-shot. The notebook provides the shuffling logic and a bar chart of prediction frequency.
sections.hint: The most recently seen example label often has outsized influence. This is called the recency bias in few-shot prompting.

notebooks.title: Agentic AI: From Reasoning to Action
notebooks.subtitle: The ReAct loop, LangChain tools, and context engineering through multi-agent isolation
learning_goals: An agent differs from a chatbot: it operates in a feedback loop, not a one-shot function.
The ReAct pattern (Reason + Act) structures this loop: observe, think, act, observe.
Tools are plain Python functions decorated with @tool; the docstring is the LLM's API contract.
Intelligence emerges from the feedback loop, not just the model's reasoning.
Context window is working memory: too much context causes degradation.
Multi-agent isolation assigns each sub-agent a clean, focused context window.

runtime.notes: Use litellm's LangChain integration (ChatLiteLLM from langchain-community) so agent code is provider-agnostic. The model string follows litellm format: `ollama/glm4:9b` for local, `ollama_chat/glm-4.7:cloud` for the ollama cloud relay. Students with API access to other providers can substitute any litellm-supported model string.

llm_config.description: Same UI panel pattern as Notebook 1: litellm model string, optional api_key, optional api_base. Default model is ollama/glm4:9b. Reactive marimo state propagates to all agent instantiations in the notebook.

sections.title: Agent vs. Chatbot: A State Machine, Not a Function
sections.content: Side-by-side diagram (ASCII or matplotlib figure): chatbot as input→output function; agent as a state machine loop. Concrete analogy: a chatbot answers 'What is the capital of France?'; an agent figures out what the question even means, fetches the data, and double-checks the answer. Key insight: the feedback loop is where intelligence lives.

sections.title: The ReAct Pattern: Reason, Then Act
sections.content: Walk through the pseudocode loop. Annotate each phase: (1) Observation — what the agent sees, (2) Thought — the chain-of-thought reasoning, (3) Action — a tool call with parameters, (4) Result — what the tool returns, which becomes the next observation. Show that the loop terminates when the agent emits a 'Final Answer' action. Reference the original ReAct paper (Yao et al., 2022) in a margin note.

sections.title: Defining Tools: The @tool Decorator
sections.content: Show three simple tools: get_current_date(), calculate(expression: str), and look_up_word(word: str). Each is a plain Python function. The @tool decorator is applied and the docstring is highlighted as the sole interface the LLM sees. An interactive cell lets students modify the docstring and observe how the agent uses the tool differently. Key point: a vague docstring produces unpredictable tool calls.

sections.title: A ReAct Agent in Action: Live Trace
sections.content: A pre-built agent with date, calculator, and word-lookup tools. A text input lets students ask any question. The notebook streams the full ReAct trace below: each Thought, Action, Action Input, and Observation on separate labeled lines with visual indentation. Students can watch the loop unfold step by step. A step counter shows how many iterations the agent took.

sections.title: Data Detective: Interrogating the Titanic Dataset
sections.content: Tools: inspect_schema() returns column names and types; sample_rows(n) returns n sample rows; query(sql: str) runs a SQL query on the DataFrame via DuckDB or pandas eval; get_summary_stats(column: str) returns mean, std, min, max, quartiles. The agent is initialized with these tools and a system prompt: 'You are a data analyst. Answer questions about the Titanic dataset by calling tools. Always verify your answer with at least one tool call.' Three pre-written question buttons seed the input: 'What was the overall survival rate?', 'Which passenger class survived at the highest rate?', 'What was the average age of survivors vs. non-survivors?'. The full ReAct trace streams below.

sections.title: Context Engineering: Multi-Agent Isolation for Literature Verification
sections.content: Five academic references are displayed (a mix of real and fabricated citations, e.g., real papers from arxiv alongside made-up DOIs and author combinations). A single monolithic agent is given all five references at once and asked to verify each. Its response is shown, including where it confuses references or fabricates verification details. Then a multi-agent architecture is demonstrated: a lead agent spawns five sub-agents, each receiving only one reference. Each sub-agent independently assesses authenticity. The lead agent collects the five assessments and produces a final report. The notebook shows the number of tokens used in each approach and the accuracy of verdicts (ground truth is provided). Key insight: isolation prevents one fabricated reference from poisoning the verification of another.
sections.implementation_notes: Use LangChain's RunnableParallel or asyncio.gather to run sub-agents concurrently. Each sub-agent has its own ChatOllama instance. The lead agent assembles a summary from the sub-agents' string outputs. The five references should be hardcoded in the notebook for reproducibility.

sections.title: Student Task: Extend the Data Detective
sections.description: Students add two new tools to the Titanic agent: (1) a cross_tabulation(row_col, col_col) tool that returns a pivot table, and (2) a filter_and_count(column, value) tool. They then prompt the agent with the multi-hop question: 'Among female passengers over 30 years old, which ticket class had the highest survival rate, and how does it compare to males in the same age group?' The agent must chain at least three tool calls to answer correctly. Success criterion: the agent produces a numerically correct answer verified by a provided ground-truth cell.
sections.hint: Write the docstring first. Describe exactly what the function returns, what the parameters mean, and what format the output is in. The LLM will call your tool exactly as described — be precise.
sections.extension: Modify the system prompt to instruct the agent to always question its first answer and run one additional verification tool call. Does this improve accuracy?

sections.title: Thought Experiment: When Does the Loop Break?
sections.description: Students deliberately give the agent a broken tool (a query function that always raises a ValueError with a vague error message). They observe how many iterations the agent attempts before giving up or hallucinating an answer. Then they improve the error message in the tool's exception to be more informative and run again. Students write a reflection: how does the quality of error messages in tool outputs affect agent behavior? This is a thought-provoking exercise connecting tool design to agent reliability.
sections.hint: Agents learn from their observations. A tool that says 'Error' is less useful than a tool that says 'ValueError: column "Age" not found. Available columns: Survived, Pclass, Name, Sex, Age, SibSp, Parch, Ticket, Fare, Cabin, Embarked.'

marimo_conventions: Each notebook must have an inline script metadata block at the top with `# /// script` format listing all dependencies so that `uvx marimo run <file>.py` installs them automatically.
Reactive marimo state (mo.state) is used for the ollama config panel so that changing credentials reruns all dependent cells without a manual restart.
Explanatory prose lives in marimo markdown cells (mo.md), not in code comments.
Student task sections use mo.callout with kind='info' or mo.md with a custom header matching the course's callout-tip style.
All LLM calls must be wrapped in try/except blocks that display a friendly error message in the notebook if the ollama endpoint is unreachable.
Streaming responses are used where possible (stream=True) so students see output appear incrementally rather than waiting for a full response.
pedagogical_conventions: Each section follows the pattern: conceptual explanation → interactive demo → reflection question.
Reflection questions are non-graded and embedded as mo.md prompts, not input widgets.
Success criteria for student tasks must be verifiable programmatically (e.g., KS test, ground-truth comparison) so students get immediate feedback.
Hints are progressive: a first hint is visible by default, and a 'reveal more' button shows a deeper hint.

implementation_decisions: Both notebooks use `litellm` for all LLM calls. litellm's unified `completion()` API accepts a model string in `provider/model` format and routes to the correct backend automatically. This means students can switch from local ollama to any cloud provider by changing a single string in the config panel.

Notebook 2 uses `ChatLiteLLM` from `langchain-community` to integrate litellm with LangChain's tool-calling and agent executor interfaces. The ReAct agent is built with langchain's `create_react_agent` and `AgentExecutor`. This keeps agent code provider-agnostic while retaining full LangChain tooling.

The Titanic dataset is loaded from a bundled CSV file (or fetched from a known public URL at notebook startup) and stored in a pandas DataFrame. DuckDB-in-process is used for the SQL query tool to avoid requiring a database server.
The multi-agent literature verification demo uses `asyncio.gather` to run sub-agents in parallel. Since marimo runs in an async context, this integrates naturally without additional threading.

The five references in the literature verification demo are hardcoded in the notebook. Three are real (with real DOIs), two are fabricated. Ground truth is a hardcoded dictionary. This ensures the demo is reproducible without internet access if needed.

All model responses that should produce numeric data (Gaussian task) are parsed with a regex that extracts all floating-point numbers from the response text, so students do not need to format the output as a list explicitly unless they choose to.
The KS test for the Gaussian task uses `scipy.stats.kstest` with `cdf='norm'`. This is displayed as a p-value with a green/red indicator.

testing_decisions.what_makes_a_good_test: Tests should verify the external behavior of helper functions (parsers, tool wrappers) without mocking internal LLM calls. LLM responses are non-deterministic; tests should cover parsing and validation logic, not the LLM output itself.
modules_to_test: Number extractor function (regex parser for the Gaussian task): test that it correctly extracts floats from various response formats.
JSON schema validator: test that valid and invalid model responses are correctly accepted or rejected.
Tool functions (inspect_schema, query, get_summary_stats, cross_tabulation, filter_and_count): test with the Titanic DataFrame that correct SQL/queries return expected results.
LLM config panel: test that changing the model string produces a litellm call with the correct model and api_base parameters.

out_of_scope: Deployment or hosting of the notebooks on a server. Students run locally with uvx.
Fine-tuning or training of any model.
Persistent memory or databases beyond the in-notebook session state.
Supporting providers beyond what litellm already handles out of the box.
further_notes: The default model ollama/glm4:9b requires ollama running locally with the glm4 model pulled. The notebook should display a clear warning and fallback suggestion (e.g., ollama/llama3.2) if the model is unavailable. For cloud access use ollama_chat/glm-4.7:cloud.

The literature verification demo references should be chosen carefully: the real papers should be well-known enough that a cloud model can verify them, and the fake papers should be plausible enough to be challenging.
Consider adding a 'Reset to defaults' button in the ollama config panel so students who accidentally misconfigure the endpoint can recover without restarting the notebook.

The CoT vs. direct answer comparison task should use a puzzle that is genuinely hard enough that the model sometimes gets it wrong without CoT. A good candidate is a riddle involving non-obvious constraint propagation or a math problem that requires two or more intermediate steps.

sprint_projects.title: Beyond Vibe-Coding: Build an ArXiv Paper Recommender the Right Way
sprint_projects.format: open-ended sprint assignment (no marimo notebook required)
sprint_projects.tool: Gemini CLI (gemini) or any agentic LLM CLI
sprint_projects.problem_statement: Vibe-coding — sending a single prompt and hoping for a working result — collapses on multi-step engineering tasks. Students experience this failure mode firsthand and apply one or more principled engineering practices to recover and succeed.
sprint_projects.task_description: Build a command-line Python tool that: (1) fetches today's new submissions from the ArXiv RSS feed for one or more categories (e.g., cs.AI, cs.LG), (2) filters papers whose title or abstract matches a student-provided interest profile (a short paragraph describing their research interests), (3) ranks the filtered papers by relevance using an LLM rating (the LLM scores each paper abstract against the interest profile on a 1-10 scale), and (4) outputs a ranked list with title, authors, score, and a one-sentence justification. The tool must handle network errors, empty feeds, and malformed XML gracefully.
sprint_projects.why_this_task: The task has enough moving parts — HTTP fetch, XML parsing, multi-step LLM calls, ranking logic, error handling — that naive vibe-coding produces incomplete or brittle code. Students who plan upfront and use principled practices finish in time; students who vibe-code hit repeated roadblocks and cannot finish.

principled_practices.name: Ralph Wiggum Loop
principled_practices.description: Before writing any code or sending any prompt to the LLM, students work through five phases: Read (understand the full task requirements), Analyze (identify the hardest sub-problems), List (enumerate all components needed), Plan (sequence the components with interfaces defined), Hypothesize (predict where failures will occur and how to handle them). Only after all five phases are complete does the student issue the first coding prompt.
principled_practices.tool_integration: Use 'gemini -p' (plan mode) to generate a structured plan. Review and refine the plan before implementation begins.
principled_practices.pedagogical_note: Named after Ralph Wiggum as a reminder that even simple-sounding tasks require structured thinking. Students who skip this phase inevitably ask the LLM for 'the whole thing at once' and get stuck.

principled_practices.name: 80/20 Plan Rule
principled_practices.description: Students should spend 80% of their total time budget planning and specifying (writing the RALPH plan, defining interfaces, writing acceptance tests) and only 20% on actual LLM-assisted implementation. For a 60-minute sprint, this means ~48 minutes of planning and ~12 minutes of implementation. The insight is that implementation goes very fast when the plan is precise — and very slowly when it is vague.
principled_practices.tool_integration: Use Gemini CLI's context file feature (--context or GEMINI.md) to write the plan into a persistent context file. The LLM implementation prompts then reference this plan rather than re-explaining everything each time.
principled_practices.pedagogical_note: Students almost always underestimate how much planning reduces total effort. The 80/20 ratio feels counterintuitive until they experience a 12-minute implementation of a previously planned feature vs. a 45-minute vibe-coding struggle.

principled_practices.name: Test-Driven Prompting
principled_practices.description: Before asking the LLM to implement any function, the student writes the acceptance test for that function. The test defines the contract: what inputs, what outputs, what edge cases. The LLM is then prompted to implement the function until the test passes. If the test fails, the student sends the failure message back to the LLM without manual debugging.
principled_practices.tool_integration: Write tests in a 'tests/' directory. Use 'gemini' to generate the implementation, then run 'python -m pytest tests/' and feed failures back to gemini.
principled_practices.pedagogical_note: This practice grounds the LLM in a concrete success criterion and prevents the 'looks right but is wrong' failure mode. Students often discover that writing the test clarifies their own understanding of the requirement.

sprint_projects.student_choice: Students may apply any combination of the three practices — or none at all (vibe-coding is explicitly allowed as a control). They are asked to track which approach they used and reflect on its effectiveness. There is no penalty for vibe-coding; the pedagogical goal is for students to experience the difference firsthand.
deliverables: Working command-line tool (python arxiv_recommender.py --interests 'your interest paragraph' --categories cs.AI,cs.LG)
A prompting log: the key prompts sent to the LLM, in order, annotated with whether they succeeded on the first attempt
A one-paragraph reflection: which principled practice(s) did you use? What would have happened if you had vibe-coded instead? What was the hardest part of the task?

evaluation.correctness: 40% — does the tool fetch, filter, rank, and output correctly?
evaluation.robustness: 20% — does it handle network errors and malformed input gracefully?
evaluation.reflection: 40% — does the reflection demonstrate genuine insight into the tradeoffs between vibe-coding and principled engineering?

gemini_cli_notes: Use 'gemini' command (Google's Gemini CLI) as the primary tool. It supports GEMINI.md context files analogous to CLAUDE.md, making it ideal for demonstrating persistent context.
Students write their RALPH plan into GEMINI.md at the project root. Every subsequent gemini invocation inherits this context, demonstrating the 'Write' strategy from the context engineering module.
Recommended model: gemini-2.0-flash for speed during the sprint.
The gemini CLI supports piping test output directly: 'python -m pytest 2>&1 | gemini fix the failing test'.
connections_to_lecture: The RALPH loop is an application of the ReAct pattern at the meta-level: students reason about the task before acting on it.
The 80/20 rule operationalizes context engineering: a well-written plan is a high-quality context injection that reduces LLM drift.
Test-driven prompting is a form of output constraint (from prompt engineering module): the test is the format specification for the LLM's implementation.
GEMINI.md is a direct demonstration of the 'Write' strategy from the context engineering module: procedural memory injected at session start.
